However, the upper bound of such performance gain is inherently constrained by the foundational capabilities of the LLMs. Therefore, scaling up the number of retrieved cases alone does not ensure consistent performance gains. Furthermore, we present the results of CASCADE’s absolute improvement over the best-performing non-parametric baseline NP-CBR in Fig. E1c. We observe that CASCADE outperforms NP-CBR in almost all settings, further demonstrating its empirical superiority.

C.5 Human-in-the-loop Results

We have shown that CASCADE can serve as a good deployment-time learner merely based on the binary feedback provided by the environment. In this subsection, we introduce two human-in-the-loop settings to demonstrate how CASCADE can benefit from human interactions to derive better performance.

Human-involved revision. In many real-world application scenarios, human experts participate in the Revise step to ensure the correctness of generated solutions (Fig. S1a). For example, in our preliminary applied work on automated software testing 21, human test engineers are responsible for revising the generated test scripts by LLMs. Under this setting, a new case can always be safely retained at each timestep; thus, the coverage gap would be closed faster due to pmin=1p_{\min}=1. We simulate this setting by retaining the case with the ground-truth label in each timestep, and make comparison between CASCADE and the best-performing non-parametric baseline NP-CBR in Fig. S1b. The results show that both CASCADE and NP-CBR benefit from human-involved revision in most settings. Meanwhile, performance deterioration can be observed in MIMIC-MR, MIMIC-MSR, CMDL, and LFD, where the retriever policies of NP-CBR and CASCADE struggle to manage the growing and diverse case bank within limited online timesteps. Importantly, CASCADE consistently outperforms NP-CBR across all settings, highlighting its superior adaptability and efficient learning of the retriever policy.

CASCADE under E-E-D retrieval framework. A notable advantage of CASCADE lies in its contextual bandit modelling of the retriever policy, which supports a human-in-the-loop setting where agents selectively seek human expert assistance within a limited budget. This is similar to the exploration-exploitation-discovery (E-E-D) framework in infinitely many-armed bandit literature 66, 49, where exploitation means selecting an apparently relevant case, exploration means selecting an uncertain case, and discovery means seeking for a novel latent case when no sufficiently relevant cases are believed to exist in the current case bank. To simulate this setting, we provide the agent with a fixed budget of 10% discovery steps across all timesteps, in which it can directly access the ground-truth label (Fig. S1c). For CASCADE, we introduce three discovery metrics: (1) Explore refers to the exploration term in Eq. (7), i.e., ∥f​(xt,c;𝝎t−1)∥𝐀t−1−1\lVert f(x_{t,c};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\rVert_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}, which reflects the uncertainty of the case. (2) Exploit refers to the exploitation term in Eq. (7), i.e., ⟨𝜽t−1,f​(xt,c;𝝎t−1)⟩\langle\bm{\theta}_{t-1},f(x_{t,c};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\rangle, which reflects the learned utility of the case. (3) UCB refers to the UCB score defined in Eq. (7), which reflects the upper confidence bound of the expected utility of the case. A discovery step is triggered when the selected metric’s value falls below a predefined threshold. Since the ranges of these metrics evolve over time, we maintain a first-in-first-out queue of length 16, and dynamically set the threshold to the 10th percentile of the queued values. As a baseline, we introduce the Random strategy, which randomly determines whether to perform discovery with a probability of 10%.

To assess these discovery strategies, we first report the percentage of counterfactual discovery advantage, i.e., the proportion of discovery steps yielding higher counterfactual rewards than exploration-exploitation actions, in Fig. S1d. As expected, the results indicate that Exploit and UCB are the best-performing discovery metrics, which consistently outperform the others in all the settings. Specifically, the Exploit and UCB strategies trigger discovery whenever no relevant cases exist in the case bank based on the learned utility function and the most optimistic estimate of expected utility, respectively. To further evaluate the benefits of the discovery mechanism, we report the relative improvement of each discovery strategy over standard CASCADE in Fig. S1e. Notably, Exploit and UCB achieve average relative performance gains of 8.83% and 8.73%, respectively, whereas Random provides only a 5.61% improvement. This demonstrates that the contextual bandit modelling in CASCADE provides an effective discovery mechanism.

Appendix D Coverage Gap Analysis

In this section, we provide the complete theoretical analyses on the coverage gap of CASCADE. To simplify the analyses, we make the following necessary assumptions:

Assumption 1.

There exists a metric space (𝒬,d𝒬)(\mathcal{Q},d_{\mathcal{Q}}) and a constant L𝒬>0L_{\mathcal{Q}}>0, such that for any query q∈𝒬q\in\mathcal{Q} and any case c=(q′,a′,r′=1)c=(q^{\prime},a^{\prime},r^{\prime}=1), we have

1−ℛ¯​(q,c)≤L𝒬⋅d𝒬​(q,q′).1-\bar{\mathcal{R}}(q,c)\leq L_{\mathcal{Q}}\cdot d_{\mathcal{Q}}(q,q^{\prime}).

Assumption 1 formalises the principle that similar problems share similar solutions, ensuring that small perturbations in the query would not lead to large variations in the expected utility for LLMs.

Assumption 2.

For query qtq_{t}, and any case c∈{(q,a,r=1)|q∈𝒬,a∈𝒜}∪∅c\in\{(q,a,r=1)|q\in\mathcal{Q},a\in\mathcal{A}\}\cup\emptyset, there exists a positive constant pmin>0p_{\min}>0 such that

ℛ¯​(qt,c)=𝔼at∼pLLM(⋅|qt,c)​[ℛ​(qt,at)]≥pmin.\bar{\mathcal{R}}(q_{t},c)=\mathbb{E}_{a_{t}\sim p_{\text{LLM}}(\cdot|q_{t},c)}[\mathcal{R}(q_{t},a_{t})]\geq p_{\min}.

Assumption 2 requires that LLMs maintain a strictly positive probability of generating a correct solution for any query, and that conditioning on any case, even an irrelevant one, does not reduce this probability to zero. We emphasise that this assumption is mainly introduced as a technical simplification to facilitate a clean theoretical analysis.

Assumption 3.

The queries {qt}t=1T\{q_{t}\}_{t=1}^{T} are independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.) draws from a distribution 𝒫\mathcal{P} over 𝒬\mathcal{Q}. There exists constants d0>0d_{0}>0, C0>0C_{0}>0, and a diameter D∈(0,∞)D\in(0,\infty) such that ∀q∈𝒬\forall q\in\mathcal{Q} and 0<ε≤D0<\varepsilon\leq D,

𝒫​(B​(q,ε))≥C0​εd0,\mathcal{P}(B(q,\varepsilon))\geq C_{0}\varepsilon^{d_{0}},

where B​(q,ε)={q′∈𝒬∣d𝒬​(q,q′)≤ε}B(q,\varepsilon)=\{q^{\prime}\in\mathcal{Q}\mid d_{\mathcal{Q}}(q,q^{\prime})\leq\varepsilon\}.

Assumption 3 models the query stream as i.i.d. draws from a distribution with a mild density condition. This is standard in non-parametric and bandit analyses and prevents degeneracy by ensuring that every metric ball has non-negligible probability mass. This assumption guarantees that similar queries recur with sufficient frequency, allowing the case bank to gradually cover the query space and making sub-linear coverage gap achievable.

Now, we can present the coverage gap bound as below.

Theorem 4.

Suppose Assumptions 1, 2, 3 hold. For any δ∈(0,1)\delta\in(0,1), define t0:=⌈8pmin​log⁡(32δ​pmin)+1⌉.t_{0}:=\left\lceil\frac{8}{p_{\min}}\log\Big(\frac{32}{\delta\,p_{\min}}\Big)+1\right\rceil. Then, with probability at least 1−δ1-\delta, the accumulated coverage gap satisfies

RTΔ≤t0+L𝒬​(2​log⁡(4​Tδ)C0​pmin)1/d0​∑t=t0+1T(t−1)−1/d0.R_{T}^{\Delta}\leq t_{0}+L_{\mathcal{Q}}\Big(\frac{2\log\big(\frac{4T}{\delta}\big)}{C_{0}p_{\min}}\Big)^{1/d_{0}}\sum_{t=t_{0}+1}^{T}(t-1)^{-1/d_{0}}.
Proof.

We first relate Δt\Delta_{t} to a nearest-neighbour distance in the case bank ℳt\mathcal{M}_{t}. Denote the distance between query qtq_{t} and the nearest query in the case bank ℳt\mathcal{M}_{t} as dt=min(q,a,1)∈ℳt⁡d𝒬​(qt,q)d_{t}=\min_{(q,a,1)\in\mathcal{M}_{t}}d_{\mathcal{Q}}(q_{t},q). According to Assumption 1, we have

Δt=ℛ​(qt,at⋆)−ℛ¯​(qt,ct⋆)≤L𝒬​dt.\Delta_{t}=\mathcal{R}(q_{t},a_{t}^{\star})-\bar{\mathcal{R}}(q_{t},c_{t}^{\star})\leq L_{\mathcal{Q}}d_{t}. (S1)

Next, we derive the high-probability lower bound on the number of the cases in the case bank, i.e., Nt=|ℳt|N_{t}=|\mathcal{M}_{t}|. By Assumption 2, at each timestep tt, we have ℙ​(rt=1∣ℱt−1)=ℛ¯​(qt,ct)≥pmin\mathbb{P}(r_{t}=1\mid\mathcal{F}_{t-1})=\bar{\mathcal{R}}(q_{t},c_{t})\geq p_{\min}. Let Ut∼Unif​(0,1)U_{t}\sim\mathrm{Unif}(0,1), realise rt=𝕀​{Ut≤pt}r_{t}=\mathbb{I}\{U_{t}\leq p_{t}\} with pt≥pminp_{t}\geq p_{\min}, and zt=𝕀​{Ut≤pmin}z_{t}=\mathbb{I}\{U_{t}\leq p_{\min}\}; thus, we have zt≤rtz_{t}\leq r_{t}. Define Zt=∑s=1t−1zs∼Binomial​(t−1,pmin)Z_{t}=\sum_{s=1}^{t-1}z_{s}\sim\mathrm{Binomial}(t-1,p_{\min}); thus, we have Nt=∑s=1t−1rs≥ZtN_{t}=\sum_{s=1}^{t-1}r_{s}\geq Z_{t}. By applying Chernoff bound to ZtZ_{t}, we have

ℙ​(Nt≤pmin​(t−1)2)≤ℙ​(Zt≤pmin​(t−1)2)≤exp⁡(−pmin​(t−1)8).\mathbb{P}\left(N_{t}\leq\frac{p_{\min}(t-1)}{2}\right)\leq\mathbb{P}\left(Z_{t}\leq\frac{p_{\min}(t-1)}{2}\right)\leq\exp\left(-\frac{p_{\min}(t-1)}{8}\right).

Then, we can derive the following union bound over t≥t0t\geq t_{0}:

ℙ(∃t≥t0:Nt≤pmin​(t−1)2)≤∑s=t0∞e−pmin​(s−1)8=e−pmin​(t0−1)/81−e−pmin/8≤16pmine−pmin​(t0−1)/8≤δ2,\mathbb{P}\left(\exists t\geq t_{0}:N_{t}\leq\frac{p_{\min}(t-1)}{2}\right)\leq\sum_{s=t_{0}}^{\infty}e^{-\frac{p_{\min}(s-1)}{8}}=\frac{e^{-p_{\min}(t_{0}-1)/8}}{1-e^{-p_{\min}/8}}\leq\frac{16}{p_{\min}}e^{-p_{\min}(t_{0}-1)/8}\leq\frac{\delta}{2},

where the second inequality is because ∀x∈(0,1),1−e−x≥x/2\forall x\in(0,1),1-e^{-x}\geq x/2. Thus, with probability at least 1−δ/21-\delta/2, we have

Nt≥pmin​(t−1)2,∀t≥t0.N_{t}\geq\frac{p_{\min}(t-1)}{2},\quad\forall t\geq t_{0}. (S2)

Next, we bound the minimum distance dtd_{t} in each timestep. Without loss of generality, we consider timestep tt with Nt≥1N_{t}\geq 1. Then, according to Assumption 3, for any ε∈(0,D]\varepsilon\in(0,D], we have

ℙ​(dt>ε)≤(1−𝒫​(B​(qt,ε)))Nt≤(1−C0​εd0)Nt≤exp⁡(−C0​Nt​εd0),\mathbb{P}(d_{t}>\varepsilon)\leq\left(1-\mathcal{P}\left(B\left(q_{t},\varepsilon\right)\right)\right)^{N_{t}}\leq(1-C_{0}\varepsilon^{d_{0}})^{N_{t}}\leq\exp(-C_{0}N_{t}\varepsilon^{d_{0}}),

where the last inequality is because ∀x∈(0,1),1−x≤e−x\forall x\in(0,1),1-x\leq e^{-x}. After choosing εt=(log⁡(2​T/δ)/C0​Nt)1/d0\varepsilon_{t}=(\log(2T/\delta)/C_{0}N_{t})^{1/d_{0}}, we have

ℙ​(dt>εt)≤δ2​T.\mathbb{P}(d_{t}>\varepsilon_{t})\leq\frac{\delta}{2T}.

A union bound over t∈[T]t\in[T] implies that with probability at least 1−δ/21-\delta/2,

dt≤(log⁡(4​Tδ)C0​Nt)1/d0,∀t∈[T]​ such that ​Nt≥1.d_{t}\leq\left(\frac{\log\big(\frac{4T}{\delta}\big)}{C_{0}N_{t}}\right)^{1/d_{0}},\qquad\forall t\in[T]\text{ such that }N_{t}\geq 1. (S3)

Now, we can derive the upper bound for accumulated coverage gap. By taking the intersection of events in Eq. (S2) and Eq. (S3), with probability at least 1−δ1-\delta, we have

RTΔ\displaystyle R_{T}^{\Delta} =∑t=1T[ℛ​(qt,at⋆)−ℛ¯​(qt,ct⋆)]\displaystyle=\sum_{t=1}^{T}[\mathcal{R}(q_{t},a_{t}^{\star})-\bar{\mathcal{R}}(q_{t},c_{t}^{\star})]
=∑t=1t0[ℛ​(qt,at⋆)−ℛ¯​(qt,ct⋆)]+∑t=t0T[ℛ​(qt,at⋆)−ℛ¯​(qt,ct⋆)]\displaystyle=\sum_{t=1}^{t_{0}}[\mathcal{R}(q_{t},a_{t}^{\star})-\bar{\mathcal{R}}(q_{t},c_{t}^{\star})]+\sum_{t=t_{0}}^{T}[\mathcal{R}(q_{t},a_{t}^{\star})-\bar{\mathcal{R}}(q_{t},c_{t}^{\star})]
≤t0+∑t=t0+1TL𝒬​dt\displaystyle\leq t_{0}+\sum_{t=t_{0}+1}^{T}L_{\mathcal{Q}}d_{t}
≤t0+∑t=t0+1TL𝒬​(log⁡(2​Tδ)C0​Nt)1/d0\displaystyle\leq t_{0}+\sum_{t=t_{0}+1}^{T}L_{\mathcal{Q}}\left(\frac{\log\big(\frac{2T}{\delta}\big)}{C_{0}N_{t}}\right)^{1/d_{0}}
≤t0+∑t=t0+1TL𝒬​(2​log⁡(2​Tδ)C0​pmin​(t−1))1/d0\displaystyle\leq t_{0}+\sum_{t=t_{0}+1}^{T}L_{\mathcal{Q}}\left(\frac{2\log\big(\frac{2T}{\delta}\big)}{C_{0}p_{\min}(t-1)}\right)^{1/d_{0}}
=t0+L𝒬​(2​log⁡(2​Tδ)C0​pmin)1/d0​∑t=t0+1T(t−1)−1/d0,\displaystyle=t_{0}+L_{\mathcal{Q}}\Big(\frac{2\log\big(\frac{2T}{\delta}\big)}{C_{0}p_{\min}}\Big)^{1/d_{0}}\sum_{t=t_{0}+1}^{T}(t-1)^{-1/d_{0}},

where the first inequality is due to Δt≤1\Delta_{t}\leq 1 and Eq. (S1), the second inequality is due to Eq. (S3), and the third inequality is due to Eq. (S2). ∎

Remark 1.

From Theorem 4, the coverage gap exhibits different scaling behaviours depending on the intrinsic dimension parameter d0d_{0}. If d0>1d_{0}>1, we can derive 𝒪~​(Td0−1d0)\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{\frac{d_{0}-1}{d_{0}}}) coverage gap bound; if 0<d0≤10<d_{0}\leq 1, we can derive 𝒪~​(1)\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(1) coverage gap bound.

Appendix E Neural-Linear Logistic Bandit: Neural-LinLogUCB

In this section, we formally introduce the proposed neural logistic bandit algorithm in CASCADE and then analyse its regret. Without loss of generality, we present theoretical analyses based on the standard neural contextual bandit with binary feedback (NCBF) setting 63, 5. For clarity, we make a slight abuse of notation during the derivation of the regret bound for the Neural-LinLogUCB algorithm. This section can be regarded as self-contained and independent from the rest of the paper.

E.1 Preliminary

Contextual Bandit with Binary Feedback. We consider the stochastic KK-armed contextual bandit problem with binary feedback 16, 37, 14, 63, 5, where the total number of timesteps TT is known. At each timestep t∈[T]t\in[T], the agent observes KK feature vectors {𝐱t,k∈ℝd∣k∈[K]}\{\mathbf{x}_{t,k}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\mid k\in[K]\} from the corresponding contextual information {xt,k∣k∈[K]}\{x_{t,k}\mid k\in[K]\}. Then, the agent selects an action ctc_{t} and receives a reward rt,ct∈{0,1}r_{t,c_{t}}\in\{0,1\}. We assume that the binary reward follows a Bernoulli distribution, where the probability of rt,ct=1r_{t,c_{t}}=1 is given by

ℙ​{rt,ct=1∣𝐱t,ct}=σ​(h​(𝐱t,ct)),\mathbb{P}\{r_{t,c_{t}}=1\mid\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}}\}=\sigma(h(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}})), (S4)

where h:ℝd→ℝh:\mathbb{R}^{d}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} is an unknown latent reward function, and σ:ℝ→[0,1]\sigma:\mathbb{R}\rightarrow[0,1] is the sigmoid function, i.e., σ​(x)=1/(1+e−x)\sigma(x)=1/(1+e^{-x}). We denote LσL_{\sigma} and κσ\kappa_{\sigma} as the Lipschitz constant and the strong monotonicity constant of the sigmoid function, i,e., κσ≤σ˙​(⋅)≤Lσ\kappa_{\sigma}\leq\dot{\sigma}(\cdot)\leq L_{\sigma}. Note that we have Lσ≤1/4L_{\sigma}\leq 1/4 for the sigmoid function.

The objective is to maximise the accumulated rewards, or equivalently minimising the following pseudo regret (or regret for short):

RT=𝔼​[∑t=1T(rt,ct⋆−rt,ct)]=∑t=1T[σ​(h​(𝐱t⋆))−σ​(h​(𝐱t,ct))],R_{T}=\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t=1}^{T}(r_{t,c_{t}^{\star}}-r_{t,c_{t}})\right]=\sum_{t=1}^{T}\left[\sigma(h(\mathbf{x}_{t}^{\star}))-\sigma(h(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}}))\right], (S5)

where ct⋆=arg⁡maxc∈[K]⁡𝔼​[rt,c]c_{t}^{\star}=\arg\max_{c\in[K]}\mathbb{E}[r_{t,c}] denotes the optimal action at timestep tt that maximises the expected reward, and 𝐱t⋆=𝐱t,ct⋆\mathbf{x}_{t}^{\star}=\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}} denotes the feature vector of the optimal action at timestep tt.

Deep Neural Networks. We follow previous works on neural contextual bandits 82, 72, 63 to utilise the fully connected network with the ReLU activation function as the network architecture. Specifically, the neural network f​(𝐱;𝝎)f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}) with depth L≥2L\geq 2 is defined as:

f​(𝐱;𝝎)=m​ϕ​(𝐖L​ϕ​(𝐖L−1​⋯​ϕ​(𝐖1​𝐱))),f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega})=\sqrt{m}\phi(\mathbf{W}_{L}\phi(\mathbf{W}_{L-1}\cdots\phi(\mathbf{W}_{1}\mathbf{x}))), (S6)

where ϕ​(x)=max⁡{0,x}\phi(x)=\max\{0,x\} is the ReLU activation function, 𝐖1∈ℝm×d\mathbf{W}_{1}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times d}, 𝐖i∈ℝm×m,2≤i≤L−1\mathbf{W}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m},2\leq i\leq L-1, 𝐖L∈ℝd×m\mathbf{W}_{L}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times m}, and 𝝎=[vec​(𝐖1)⊤,⋯,vec​(𝐖L)⊤]⊤∈ℝp\bm{\omega}=[\text{vec}(\mathbf{W}_{1})^{\top},\cdots,\text{vec}(\mathbf{W}_{L})^{\top}]^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{p} with p=2​m​d+m2​(L−2)p=2md+m^{2}(L-2). We denote the gradient of the neural network by 𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎)=∇𝝎f​(𝐱;𝝎)∈ℝd×p\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega})=\nabla_{\bm{\omega}}f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega})\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times p}.

E.2 Neural-Linear Logistic Bandit Algorithm

Modelling Reward Function with Deep Representation Model and Shallow Linear Head. Although neural logistic bandit algorithms have shown powerful capabilities in approximating the complex reward function 82, 63, 5, they are inefficient at estimating uncertainty due to the large number of parameters involved in the entire network. To this end, we follow previous works 53, 76, 72 to decouple representation learning and uncertainty estimation, and propose the neural linear logistic upper confidence bound (Neural-LinLogUCB) algorithm. In particular, we model the reward function with a deep neural network model f​(⋅;𝝎)f(\cdot;\bm{\omega}) for representation learning and a shallow linear head 𝜽∈ℝd\bm{\theta}\in\mathbb{R}^{d} for uncertainty estimation, i.e., h​(𝐱)=⟨𝜽⋆,f​(𝐱;𝝎⋆)⟩h(\mathbf{x})=\langle\bm{\theta}^{\star},f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}^{\star})\rangle, where 𝜽⋆\bm{\theta}^{\star} and 𝝎⋆\bm{\omega}^{\star} denote the unknown optimal parameters for linear head and neural network respectively. This decoupling enables the algorithm to benefit from both strong approximation capabilities of deep neural networks for representation learning and efficient exploration from the shallow linear head.

Initialisation. We follow the standard initialisation scheme from neural contextual bandits 82, 72, 63, 5, where each entry of 𝝎\bm{\omega} follows an appropriate Gaussian distribution. Specifically, for any layer 1≤l≤L−11\leq l\leq L-1, we set 𝐖l=[𝐖𝟎𝟎𝐖]\mathbf{W}_{l}=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{W}&\mathbf{0}\\ \mathbf{0}&\mathbf{W}\end{bmatrix}, where the parameters in 𝐖\mathbf{W} is independently sampled from the Gaussian distribution 𝒩​(0,4/m)\mathcal{N}(0,4/m); for the last layer LL, we set 𝐖L=[𝐖⊤−𝐖⊤]\mathbf{W}_{L}=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{W}^{\top}&-\mathbf{W}^{\top}\end{bmatrix}, where the parameters in 𝐖\mathbf{W} is independently sampled from the Gaussian distribution 𝒩​(0,2/m)\mathcal{N}(0,2/m). For the linear head 𝜽\bm{\theta}, we initialise its each entry by independently sampling from the Gaussian distribution 𝒩​(0,1/d)\mathcal{N}(0,1/d).

Learning Algorithm. In timestep tt, the agent observes a set of feature vectors 𝒳t={𝐱t,1,…,𝐱t,K}\mathcal{X}_{t}=\{\mathbf{x}_{t,1},...,\mathbf{x}_{t,K}\}. Then, it selects the action that maximises the following upper confidence bound:

ct=arg⁡maxk∈[K]⁡{⟨𝜽t−1,f​(𝐱t,k;𝝎t−1)⟩+αt​∥f​(𝐱t,k;𝝎t−1)∥𝐀t−1−1},c_{t}=\arg\max_{k\in[K]}\left\{\langle\bm{\theta}_{t-1},f(\mathbf{x}_{t,k};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\rangle+\alpha_{t}\lVert f(\mathbf{x}_{t,k};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\rVert_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}\right\}, (S7)

where {αt>0}t=1T\{\alpha_{t}>0\}_{t=1}^{T} are exploration coefficients, and the matrix 𝐀t\mathbf{A}_{t} is defined as

𝐀t=λ​𝐈+∑s=1tf​(𝐱s,cs;𝝎s−1)​f​(𝐱s,cs;𝝎s−1)⊤,\mathbf{A}_{t}=\lambda\mathbf{I}+\sum_{s=1}^{t}f(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}};\bm{\omega}_{s-1})f(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}};\bm{\omega}_{s-1})^{\top}, (S8)

where λ>0\lambda>0 denotes the hyper-parameter of the regularization strength in linear head estimation. After selecting the action ctc_{t}, the agent will receive a stochastic binary reward rtr_{t}.

Then, we utilise the current history data {(𝐱s,cs,rs)}s=1t\{(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}},r_{s})\}_{s=1}^{t} to learn the neural network and linear head. First, we estimate the linear head by solving the the following logistic regression problem with L2 regularisation:

min𝜽∈ℝd​∑s=1t[−rs​log⁡σ​(𝜽⊤​f​(𝐱s,cs;𝝎s−1))−(1−rs)​log⁡(1−σ​(𝜽⊤​f​(𝐱s,cs;𝝎s−1)))]+λ2​∥𝜽∥22.\min_{\bm{\theta}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}}\sum_{s=1}^{t}\left[-r_{s}\log\sigma\left(\bm{\theta}^{\top}f(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}};\bm{\omega}_{s-1})\right)-(1-r_{s})\log\left(1-\sigma\left(\bm{\theta}^{\top}f(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}};\bm{\omega}_{s-1})\right)\right)\right]+\frac{\lambda}{2}\lVert\bm{\theta}\rVert^{2}_{2}. (S9)

Then, we train the neural network by minimising the following cross-entropy loss function:

ℒt​(𝝎)=1m​∑s=1t[−rs​log⁡σ​(𝜽s−1⊤​f​(𝐱s,cs;𝝎))−(1−rs)​log⁡(1−σ​(𝜽s−1⊤​f​(𝐱s,cs;𝝎)))]+λ2​∥𝝎−𝝎0∥22.\mathcal{L}_{t}(\bm{\omega})=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{s=1}^{t}\left[-r_{s}\log\sigma\left(\bm{\theta}_{s-1}^{\top}f(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}};\bm{\omega})\right)-(1-r_{s})\log\left(1-\sigma\left(\bm{\theta}_{s-1}^{\top}f(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}};\bm{\omega})\right)\right)\right]+\frac{\lambda}{2}\lVert\bm{\omega}-\bm{\omega}_{0}\rVert_{2}^{2}. (S10)

We summarise the theoretical version of the algorithm in Algorithm 2.

E.3 Regret Analysis

Now, we analyse the upper regret bound of the proposed algorithm Neural-LogUCB. Our regret analysis follows existing neural contextual bandit works 82, 72, 63 built upon the neural tangent kernel (NTK) matrix 26.

Definition 1.

Let {𝐱i}i=1T​K\{\mathbf{x}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{TK} be a set of feature vectors. Define

𝚺~i,j(1)=𝚺i,j(1)=⟨𝐱i,𝐱j⟩,\tilde{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_{i,j}^{(1)}=\mathbf{\Sigma}_{i,j}^{(1)}=\langle\mathbf{x}_{i},\mathbf{x}_{j}\rangle,
𝚲i,j(l)=[𝚺i,i(l)𝚺i,j(l)𝚺j,i(l)𝚺j,j(l)],\mathbf{\Lambda}_{i,j}^{(l)}=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{\Sigma}_{i,i}^{(l)}&\mathbf{\Sigma}_{i,j}^{(l)}\\ \mathbf{\Sigma}_{j,i}^{(l)}&\mathbf{\Sigma}_{j,j}^{(l)}\end{bmatrix},
𝚺i,j(l+1)=2​𝔼u,v∼𝒩​(0,𝚲i,j(l))​[ϕ​(u)​ϕ​(v)],\mathbf{\Sigma}_{i,j}^{(l+1)}=2\mathbb{E}_{u,v\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\mathbf{\Lambda}_{i,j}^{(l)})}[\phi(u)\phi(v)],
𝚺~i,j(l+1)=2​𝚺~i,j(l)​𝔼u,v∼𝒩​(0,𝚲i,j(l))​[ϕ˙​(u)​ϕ˙​(v)]+𝚺i,j(l).\tilde{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_{i,j}^{(l+1)}=2\tilde{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_{i,j}^{(l)}\mathbb{E}_{u,v\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\mathbf{\Lambda}_{i,j}^{(l)})}[\dot{\phi}(u)\dot{\phi}(v)]+\mathbf{\Sigma}_{i,j}^{(l)}.

Then, the neural tangent kernel matrix on this set of feature vectors is defined as

𝐇=(𝚺~(L)+𝚺(L))/2.\mathbf{H}=(\tilde{\mathbf{\Sigma}}^{(L)}+\mathbf{\Sigma}^{(L)})/2.

Now, we introduce several necessary standard assumptions in neural contextual bandits 82, 72, 63, 5.

Assumption 4.

∀t∈[T]\forall t\in[T], ∀k∈[K]\forall k\in[K], we assume that ∥𝐱t,k∥2=1\lVert\mathbf{x}_{t,k}\rVert_{2}=1 and each entry satisfies that [𝐱t,k]j=[𝐱t,k]j+d/2[\mathbf{x}_{t,k}]_{j}=[\mathbf{x}_{t,k}]_{j+d/2}.

The first assumption is quite mild: the first part is only introduced for convenience, while the second part can be easily satisfied if we construct a new feature vector 𝐱′=(𝐱⊤,𝐱⊤)⊤/2\mathbf{x}^{\prime}=(\mathbf{x}^{\top},\mathbf{x}^{\top})^{\top}/\sqrt{2}. With this assumption, we can derive that the initialisation scheme above leads to f​(𝐱t,k;𝝎0)=𝟎f(\mathbf{x}_{t,k};\bm{\omega}_{0})=\mathbf{0} for all t∈[T],k∈[K]t\in[T],k\in[K].

Assumption 5.

There exists a constant ℓLip>0\ell_{\mathrm{Lip}}>0 such that for all 𝐱,𝐱′∈{𝐱i}i=1T​K\mathbf{x},\mathbf{x}^{\prime}\in\{\mathbf{x}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{TK},

∥∇𝝎f​(𝐱;𝝎0)−∇𝝎f​(𝐱′;𝝎0)∥≤ℓLip​∥𝐱−𝐱′∥2.\lVert\nabla_{\bm{\omega}}f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{0})-\nabla_{\bm{\omega}}f(\mathbf{x}^{\prime};\bm{\omega}_{0})\rVert\leq\ell_{\text{Lip}}\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{\prime}\rVert_{2}.

This assumption ensures the stability of the parameter gradients with respect to input variations, which can be satisfied when T​KTK feature vectors lie in a certain subspace of ℝd\mathbb{R}^{d}.

Assumption 6.

The strong monotonicity constant is positive, i.e., κσ>0\kappa_{\sigma}>0.

Finally, we introduce the assumption on the NTK matrix as below.

Assumption 7.

The NTK matrix 𝐇\mathbf{H} is positive definite, i.e., there exists a constant λ0>0\lambda_{0}>0, such that 𝐇⪰λ0​𝐈\mathbf{H}\succeq\lambda_{0}\mathbf{I}.

This assumption is also widely introduced by existing works, which requires the NTK matrix to be non-singular.

Now, we can present the regret bound of the proposed algorithm Neural-LinLogUCB.

Theorem 5.

Suppose Assumptions 4, 5, 6, 7 hold. Assume that ‖𝛉⋆‖2≤M\|\bm{\theta}^{\star}\|_{2}\leq M for some positive constant M>0M>0. For any δ∈(0,1)\delta\in(0,1), choose αt\alpha_{t} in Neural-LinLogUCB as

αt=1κσ​(ν​2​(d​log⁡(1+L​t2/λ)+log⁡(1/δ))+λ​M).\alpha_{t}=\frac{1}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\Big(\nu\sqrt{2(d\log(1+Lt^{2}/\lambda)+\log(1/\delta))}+\sqrt{\lambda}M\Big).

For m≥p​o​l​y​(L,d,1/κσ,Lσ,log⁡(1/δ),log⁡(T​K))m\geq poly(L,d,1/\kappa_{\sigma},L_{\sigma},\log(1/\delta),\log(TK)), with probability at least 1−δ1-\delta, we have

RT\displaystyle R_{T} ≤C1κσ​T​d​log⁡(1+L​T2/λ2)​(ν​d​log⁡(1+L​T2/λ)+log⁡(1/δ)+λ​M)\displaystyle\leq\frac{C_{1}}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\sqrt{Td\log(1+LT^{2}/\lambda^{2})}\Big(\nu\sqrt{d\log(1+LT^{2}/\lambda)+\log(1/\delta)}+\sqrt{\lambda}M\Big)
+C2​dκσ​log⁡(1/δ)​L​‖𝐡−𝐡~‖𝐇−1\displaystyle\quad+\frac{C_{2}d}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)L}\|\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}}\|_{\mathbf{H}^{-1}}
⋅(m−1/6​T5/3​d​log⁡m​λ−2/3​L3+ℓLip​m−1/2​T3/2​λ−1/2),\displaystyle\quad\cdot\Big(m^{-1/6}T^{5/3}\sqrt{d\log m}\lambda^{-2/3}L^{3}+\ell_{\text{Lip}}m^{-1/2}T^{3/2}\lambda^{-1/2}\Big),

where C1,C2>0C_{1},C_{2}>0 are absolute constants, 𝐡=(h​(𝐱1),h​(𝐱2),⋯,h​(𝐱T​K))⊤∈ℝT​K\mathbf{h}=\big(h(\mathbf{x}_{1}),h(\mathbf{x}_{2}),\cdots,h(\mathbf{x}_{TK})\big)^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{TK} and 𝐡~=(𝛉0⊤​f​(𝐱1;𝛚0),⋯,𝛉T−1⊤​f​(𝐱T​K;𝛚T−1))⊤∈ℝT​K\tilde{\mathbf{h}}=\big(\bm{\theta}_{0}^{\top}f(\mathbf{x}_{1};\bm{\omega}_{0}),\cdots,\bm{\theta}_{T-1}^{\top}f(\mathbf{x}_{TK};\bm{\omega}_{T-1})\big)^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{TK}.

Remark 2.

With the same conditions of Theorem 5, let BB be a constant such that ‖𝐡−𝐡~‖𝐇−1≤B\|\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}}\|_{\mathbf{H}^{-1}}\leq B; if we choose a sufficiently wide neural network such that m≥T7m\geq T^{7}, the regret of Neural-LinLogUCB is RT=𝒪~​(Bκσ​T)R_{T}=\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\frac{B}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\sqrt{T}).

Comparison with Other Neural Contextual Bandits. The regret bound of the proposed Neural-LinLogUCB is worse than that of Neural-LinUCB 72 𝒪​(B​T)\mathcal{O}(B\sqrt{T}) with the additional dependency of κσ\kappa_{\sigma}. The reason for this gap is analogous to why NCBF-UCB 63 achieves a worse regret bound than Neural-UCB 82: both arise from the fundamental difference between binary feedback and continuous feedback settings.

Practical Implementation of Neural-LinLogUCB in CASCADE. Due to the complexity of the natural-language context space, we adapt the theoretical Neural-LinLogUCB framework in CASCADE and introduce several modifications for practical implementation.

Firstly, to derive the feature vectors 𝐱\mathbf{x} from the context information xx (i.e., the concatenation of the query and the case), one may apply a pretrained embedding model to convert the discrete natural language into continuous vector representation. However, prior work in the information retrieval community 45 has shown that learning complex interactions via MLPs on top of such frozen embeddings can be challenging, particularly in online learning settings. Thus, we replace the MLPs in Neural-LinLogUCB by a Transformer-based encoder model that is initialised from gte-reranker-modernbert-base 80 to benefit from its complex interaction modelling capabilities and good pretrained representations. Meanwhile, we initialise the linear head 𝜽\bm{\theta} with the last layer of gte-reranker-modernbert-base, ensuring that the initial behaviour of the reward model aligns with that of the pretrained reranker model.

Secondly, Neural-LinLogUCB requires updating both the linear head and the deep neural network at every timestep, which can be computationally expensive. To mitigate this overhead, we apply stochastic gradient descent to update the linear head at each step, serving as a computationally efficient approximation to solving the full logistic regression problem. For the encoder, we further reduce the cost by performing only a single-step mini-batch gradient descent update every HH timesteps, which maintains efficiency while still allowing the model to adapt over time.

These modifications enable Neural-LinLogUCB a practical contextual bandit solution for learning the retriever model in the case-based reasoning framework.

E.4 Proof of the Main Results

In this subsection, we provide the proof of the regret bound for Neural-LinLogUCB, which basically leverages the techniques from previous neural contextual bandits 82, 72, 63. Firstly, we present several useful lemmas from them.

Lemma 1 (Lemma C.1 from Xu et al. 72).

There exists 𝛚⋆∈ℝp\bm{\omega}^{\star}\in\mathbb{R}^{p} such that m​∥𝛚⋆−𝛚(0)∥2≤(𝐡−𝐡~)⊤​𝐇−1​(𝐡−𝐡~)≤B\sqrt{m}\lVert\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}^{(0)}\rVert_{2}\leq\sqrt{(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})^{\top}\mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})}\leq B and it holds that

h​(𝐱t,k)=𝜽⋆⊤​f​(𝐱t,k;𝝎t−1)+𝜽0⊤​𝐠​(𝐱t,k;𝝎0)​(𝝎⋆−𝝎0),h(\mathbf{x}_{t,k})=\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}f(\mathbf{x}_{t,k};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})+\bm{\theta}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,k};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0}),

for all k∈[K]k\in[K] and t∈[T]t\in[T].

Lemma 1 implies that the reward function at points 𝐱\mathbf{x} can be approximated by a linear function around the initial parameter 𝝎0\bm{\omega}_{0}.

The next lemma show the upper bound on the distance between 𝝎t\bm{\omega}_{t} and 𝝎0\bm{\omega}_{0}.

Lemma 2 (Lemma 3 from Verma et al. 63).

We have that ∥𝛚t−𝛚0∥2≤t/(m​λ),∀t∈[T]\lVert\bm{\omega}_{t}-\bm{\omega}_{0}\rVert_{2}\leq\sqrt{t/(m\lambda)},\forall t\in[T].

With Lemma 2, we can derive the following lemmas regarding the gradients of the neural networks.

Lemma 3 (Lemma 4 from Verma et al. 63, Lemma B.5 and Lemma B.6 from Zhou et al. 82).

Let τ≜2​t/(m​λ)\tau\triangleq 2\sqrt{t/(m\lambda)}. There exists constants C1,C2>0C_{1},C_{2}>0 such that with probability of at least 1−δ1-\delta,

∥𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎t)∥F≤C1​m​L​d,\lVert\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{t})\rVert_{F}\leq C_{1}\sqrt{mLd},
∥𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎0)−𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎t)∥F≤C2​m​d​log⁡m​τ1/3​L7/2=C2​m1/3​d​log⁡m​(tλ)1/6​L7/2,\lVert\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{0})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{t})\rVert_{F}\leq C_{2}\sqrt{md\log m}\tau^{1/3}L^{7/2}=C_{2}m^{1/3}\sqrt{d\log m}\Big(\frac{t}{\lambda}\Big)^{1/6}L^{7/2},

for any 𝐱∈{𝐱i}i=1T​K\mathbf{x}\in\{\mathbf{x}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{TK}.

Furthermore, we have the following lemma to bound the distance between the output of neural network and its linearisation.

Lemma 4 (Lemma C.3 from Xu et al.72, Lemma 5 from Verma et al. 63, Lemma B.4 from Zhou et al. 82).

Let τ≜2​t/(m​λ)\tau\triangleq 2\sqrt{t/(m\lambda)}. There exists constants C3>0C_{3}>0 such that with probability of at least 1−δ1-\delta,

∥f​(𝐱;𝝎t)−𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎0)​(𝝎t−𝝎0)∥2≤C3​τ4/3​L3​d​m​log⁡m=C3​m−1/6​d​log⁡m​(tλ)2/3​L3,\lVert f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{t})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}_{t}-\bm{\omega}_{0})\rVert_{2}\leq C_{3}\tau^{4/3}L^{3}\sqrt{dm\log m}=C_{3}m^{-1/6}\sqrt{d\log m}\Big(\frac{t}{\lambda}\Big)^{2/3}L^{3},

for any 𝐱∈{𝐱i}i=1T​K\mathbf{x}\in\{\mathbf{x}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{TK}.

In addition, we can derive the following lemma regarding the bound of the learned representation vectors.

Lemma 5.

There exists constants C3>0C_{3}>0 such that with probability of at least 1−δ1-\delta,

∥f​(𝐱;𝝎t)∥2≤C3​L​d​t/λ,\lVert f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{t})\rVert_{2}\leq C_{3}\sqrt{Ldt/\lambda},

for any 𝐱∈{𝐱i}i=1T​K\mathbf{x}\in\{\mathbf{x}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{TK}.

Proof.
∥f​(𝐱;𝝎t)∥2\displaystyle\lVert f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{t})\rVert_{2} =∥f​(𝐱;𝝎t)−𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎0)​(𝝎t−𝝎0)+𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎0)​(𝝎t−𝝎0)∥2\displaystyle=\lVert f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{t})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}_{t}-\bm{\omega}_{0})+\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}_{t}-\bm{\omega}_{0})\rVert_{2}
≤∥f​(𝐱;𝝎t)−𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎0)​(𝝎t−𝝎0)∥2+∥𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎0)​(𝝎t−𝝎0)∥2\displaystyle\leq\lVert f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{t})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}_{t}-\bm{\omega}_{0})\rVert_{2}+\lVert\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}_{t}-\bm{\omega}_{0})\rVert_{2}
≤∥f​(𝐱;𝝎t)−𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎0)​(𝝎t−𝝎0)∥2+∥𝐠​(𝐱;𝝎0)∥F⋅∥𝝎t−𝝎0∥2\displaystyle\leq\lVert f(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{t})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}_{t}-\bm{\omega}_{0})\rVert_{2}+\lVert\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x};\bm{\omega}_{0})\rVert_{F}\cdot\lVert\bm{\omega}_{t}-\bm{\omega}_{0}\rVert_{2}
≤C3​m−1/6​d​log⁡m​(tλ)2/3​L3+C1​m​L​d​t/(m​λ)\displaystyle\leq C_{3}m^{-1/6}\sqrt{d\log m}\Big(\frac{t}{\lambda}\Big)^{2/3}L^{3}+C_{1}\sqrt{mLd}\sqrt{t/(m\lambda)}
≤C3​L​d​t/λ,\displaystyle\leq C_{3}\sqrt{Ldt/\lambda},

where the first inequality is due to triangle inequality, the second inequality is due to Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, the third inequality comes from Lemma 4, Lemma 3 and Lemma 2, and the last inequality can be achieved by a large enough mm. ∎

Next, we introduce several lemmas related to the matrix 𝐀t\mathbf{A}_{t}.

Lemma 6 (Lemma C.6 in Xu et al. 72, Lemma 10 and Lemma 11 from Abbasi-Yadkori et al. 2).

Let {𝐱t}t=1∞\{\mathbf{x}_{t}\}_{t=1}^{\infty} be a sequence in ℝd\mathbb{R}^{d} and λ>0\lambda>0. Suppose ‖𝐱t‖2≤G\|\mathbf{x}_{t}\|_{2}\leq G and λ≥max⁡{1,G2}\lambda\geq\max\{1,G^{2}\} for some G>0G>0, Let 𝐀t=λ​𝐈+∑s=1t𝐱t​𝐱t⊤\mathbf{A}_{t}=\lambda\mathbf{I}+\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathbf{x}_{t}\mathbf{x}_{t}^{\top}. Then, we have

det(𝐀t)≤(λ+t​G2/d)d,\det(\mathbf{A}_{t})\leq(\lambda+tG^{2}/d)^{d},
∑t=1T‖𝐱t‖𝐀t−1−12≤2​log⁡det𝐀Tdetλ​𝐈≤2​d​log⁡(1+T​G2/(λ​d)).\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|\mathbf{x}_{t}\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}^{2}\leq 2\log\frac{\det{\mathbf{A}_{T}}}{\det\lambda\mathbf{I}}\leq 2d\log(1+TG^{2}/(\lambda d)).
Lemma 7 (Theorem 1 in Abbasi-Yadkori et al. 35).

Let {ξt}t=1∞\{\xi_{t}\}_{t=1}^{\infty} be a real-valued stochastic process and {𝐱t}t=1∞\{\mathbf{x}_{t}\}_{t=1}^{\infty} be a stochastic process in ℝd\mathbb{R}^{d}. Let ℱt=σ​(𝐱1,⋯,𝐱t+1,ξ1,⋯,ξt)\mathcal{F}_{t}=\sigma(\mathbf{x}_{1},\cdots,\mathbf{x}_{t+1},\xi_{1},\cdots,\xi_{t}) be a σ\sigma-algebra, such that 𝐱t\mathbf{x}_{t} and ξt\xi_{t} are ℱt−1\mathcal{F}_{t-1}-measurable. Let 𝐀t=λ​𝐈+∑s=1t𝐱t​𝐱t⊤\mathbf{A}_{t}=\lambda\mathbf{I}+\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathbf{x}_{t}\mathbf{x}_{t}^{\top} for some constant λ>0\lambda>0 and 𝐬t=∑t=1Tξt​𝐱t\mathbf{s}_{t}=\sum_{t=1}^{T}\xi_{t}\mathbf{x}_{t}. If ξt\xi_{t} is ν\nu-sub-Gaussian conditioned on ℱt−1\mathcal{F}_{t-1}, then for any η∈(0,1)\eta\in(0,1), with probability at least 1−δ1-\delta, we have

‖𝐬t‖𝐀t−12≤2​ν2​log⁡det(𝐀t)1/2​det(λ​𝐈)−1/2δ.\|\mathbf{s}_{t}\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t}^{-1}}^{2}\leq 2\nu^{2}\log\frac{\det(\mathbf{A}_{t})^{1/2}\det(\lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1/2}}{\delta}.
Lemma 8 (Lemma C.5 from Verma et al. 72).

Assume that 𝐀t=λ​𝐈+∑s=1tϕs​ϕs⊤\mathbf{A}_{t}=\lambda\mathbf{I}+\sum_{s=1}^{t}\bm{\phi}_{s}\bm{\phi}_{s}^{\top}, where ϕ∈ℝd\bm{\phi}\in\mathbb{R}^{d} and for λ,G>0\lambda,G>0, ‖ϕs‖2≤G,∀s∈[t]\|\bm{\phi}_{s}\|_{2}\leq G,\forall s\in[t]. Let {ζt}t=1,⋯\{\zeta_{t}\}_{t=1,\cdots} be a real-value sequence such that |ζt|≤U|\zeta_{t}|\leq U for some constant U>0U>0. Then, we have

‖𝐀t−1​∑s=1tϕs​ζs‖2≤2​U​d,∀t=1,2,⋯\Big\|\mathbf{A}_{t}^{-1}\sum_{s=1}^{t}\bm{\phi}_{s}\zeta_{s}\Big\|_{2}\leq 2Ud,\forall t=1,2,\cdots

Now, we start the proof by deriving the confidence bound of the estimated 𝜽t\bm{\theta}_{t}. Different from LinUCB 35 to directly bound ‖𝜽t−𝜽⋆‖𝐀t\|\bm{\theta}_{t}-\bm{\theta}^{\star}\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t}}, we need to consider the bias caused by representation learning via the deep neural networks. Otherwise, we would derive the linear regret bound.

Specifically, let 𝐳s=f​(𝐱s,cs;𝝎s−1)∈ℝd\mathbf{z}_{s}=f(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}};\bm{\omega}_{s-1})\in\mathbb{R}^{d} be the representation vectors at timestep ss, and we have 𝐀t=λ​𝐈+∑s=1t𝐳s​𝐳s⊤\mathbf{A}_{t}=\lambda\mathbf{I}+\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathbf{z}_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}^{\top}. By Lemma 1, the underlying reward function can be rewritten as

h​(𝐱t,k)=𝜽⋆⊤​f​(𝐱t,k;𝝎t−1)+𝜽0⊤​𝐠​(𝐱t,k;𝝎0)​(𝝎⋆−𝝎0).h(\mathbf{x}_{t,k})=\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}f(\mathbf{x}_{t,k};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})+\bm{\theta}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,k};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0}).

For brevity, let bs=b​(𝐱s,cs)=𝜽0⊤​𝐠​(𝐱s,cs;𝝎0)​(𝝎⋆−𝝎0)b_{s}=b(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}})=\bm{\theta}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0}) denote the bias induced by the mismatch between the learned and true representation parameters, and δs=σ​(𝜽⋆⊤​𝐳s+bs)−σ​(𝜽⋆⊤​𝐳s)\delta_{s}=\sigma\big(\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}\mathbf{z}_{s}+b_{s}\big)-\sigma\big(\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}\mathbf{z}_{s}\big) as the corresponding bias after applying the sigmoid function.

According to Algorithm 2, we solve the regularised logistic regression loss at each timestep tt as:

ℓt​(𝜽)=∑s=1t[−rs​log⁡σ​(𝜽⊤​𝐳s)−(1−rs)​log⁡(1−σ​(𝜽⊤​𝐳s))]+λ2​∥𝜽∥22.\ell_{t}(\bm{\theta})=\sum_{s=1}^{t}\left[-r_{s}\log\sigma\left(\bm{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{z}_{s}\right)-(1-r_{s})\log\left(1-\sigma\left(\bm{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{z}_{s}\right)\right)\right]+\frac{\lambda}{2}\lVert\bm{\theta}\rVert^{2}_{2}.

We derive the estimator of 𝜽t\bm{\theta}_{t} as the minimiser of the loss function above, i.e., 𝜽t=arg⁡min𝜽∈ℝd⁡ℓt​(𝜽)\bm{\theta}_{t}=\arg\min_{\bm{\theta}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}}\ell_{t}(\bm{\theta}), where the optimality condition is ∇𝜽ℓt​(𝜽)=𝟎\nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\ell_{t}(\bm{\theta})=\mathbf{0}, i.e.,

λ​𝜽t+∑s=1t(σ​(𝜽t⊤​𝐳s)−rs)​𝐳s=𝟎.\lambda\bm{\theta}_{t}+\sum_{s=1}^{t}\Big(\sigma(\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\top}\mathbf{z}_{s})-r_{s}\Big)\mathbf{z}_{s}=\mathbf{0}.

Let ϵs\epsilon_{s} be the observation noise in timestep ss, i.e.,

rs=σ​(𝜽⋆⊤​𝐳s+bs)+ϵs=σ​(𝜽⋆⊤​𝐳s)+δs+ϵs.r_{s}=\sigma(\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}\mathbf{z}_{s}+b_{s})+\epsilon_{s}=\sigma(\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}\mathbf{z}_{s})+\delta_{s}+\epsilon_{s}.

Let λ′∈(0,1)\lambda^{\prime}\in(0,1), and let ξt,s=λ′​𝜽t⊤​𝐳s+(1−λ′)​𝜽⋆⊤​𝐳s\xi_{t,s}=\lambda^{\prime}\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\top}\mathbf{z}_{s}+(1-\lambda^{\prime})\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}\mathbf{z}_{s}. Then, according to the mean-value theorem, we have for some λ′\lambda^{\prime} that

σ​(𝜽t⊤​𝐳s)−σ​(𝜽⋆⊤​𝐳s)=σ˙​(ξt,s)​𝐳s⊤​(𝜽t−𝜽⋆),\sigma(\bm{\theta}_{t}^{\top}\mathbf{z}_{s})-\sigma(\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}\mathbf{z}_{s})=\dot{\sigma}(\xi_{t,s})\mathbf{z}_{s}^{\top}(\bm{\theta}_{t}-\bm{\theta}^{\star}),

where κσ≤σ˙​(ξt,s)≤Lσ\kappa_{\sigma}\leq\dot{\sigma}(\xi_{t,s})\leq L_{\sigma}. This allows us to show that

(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)​(𝜽t−𝜽⋆)=∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s+∑s=1tδs​𝐳s−λ​𝜽⋆,\big(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t}\big)(\bm{\theta}_{t}-\bm{\theta}^{\star})=\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}+\sum_{s=1}^{t}\delta_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}-\lambda\bm{\theta}^{\star}, (S11)

where the matrix 𝐀^t\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t} is defined as:

𝐀^t=∑s=1tσ˙​(ξt,s)​𝐳s​𝐳s⊤.\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t}=\sum_{s=1}^{t}\dot{\sigma}(\xi_{t,s})\mathbf{z}_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}^{\top}.

Then, we define the bias term as 𝐛t=(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1​∑s=1tδs​𝐳s\mathbf{b}_{t}=(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1}\sum_{s=1}^{t}\delta_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}, and introduce the following bias-corrected confidence bound.

Lemma 9.

For any δ∈(0,1)\delta\in(0,1), with probability at least 1−δ1-\delta, the distance between the estimated 𝛉t\bm{\theta}_{t} and 𝛉⋆\bm{\theta}^{\star} can be bounded as follows:

‖𝜽t−𝜽⋆−𝐛t‖𝐀t≤1κσ​(ν​2​(d​log⁡(1+L​t2/λ)+log⁡(1/δ))+λ​M).\big\|\bm{\theta}_{t}-\bm{\theta}^{\star}-\mathbf{b}_{t}\big\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t}}\leq\frac{1}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\Big(\nu\sqrt{2(d\log(1+Lt^{2}/\lambda)+\log(1/\delta))}+\sqrt{\lambda}M\Big). (S12)
Proof.

We first show that λ​𝐈+𝐀^t\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t} is spectrally sandwiched between κσ​𝐀t\kappa_{\sigma}\mathbf{A}_{t} and 𝐀t\mathbf{A}_{t}. Specifically, by the definition of strong monotonicity constant of sigmoid function, i.e., κσ≤σ˙​(⋅)≤1\kappa_{\sigma}\leq\dot{\sigma}(\cdot)\leq 1, we have

λ​𝐈+𝐀^t⪰λ​𝐈+κσ​∑s=1t𝐳s​𝐳s⊤⪰κσ​(λ​𝐈+∑s=1t𝐳s​𝐳s⊤)=κσ​𝐀t.\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t}\succeq\lambda\mathbf{I}+\kappa_{\sigma}\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathbf{z}_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}^{\top}\succeq\kappa_{\sigma}\big(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathbf{z}_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}^{\top}\big)=\kappa_{\sigma}\mathbf{A}_{t}.

Also, since 𝐀^t⪯∑s=1t𝐳s​𝐳s⊤=𝐀t−λ​𝐈\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t}\preceq\sum_{s=1}^{t}\mathbf{z}_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}^{\top}=\mathbf{A}_{t}-\lambda\mathbf{I}, we have

λ​𝐈+𝐀^t⪯λ​𝐈+(𝐀t−λ​𝐈)=𝐀t.\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t}\preceq\lambda\mathbf{I}+(\mathbf{A}_{t}-\lambda\mathbf{I})=\mathbf{A}_{t}.

Combining both, we can derive that

κσ​𝐀t⪯λ​𝐈+𝐀^t⪯𝐀t.\kappa_{\sigma}\mathbf{A}_{t}\preceq\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t}\preceq\mathbf{A}_{t}. (S13)

Then, we use the definition of 𝐛t\mathbf{b}_{t} to rewrite Eq. (S11) as:

𝜽t−𝜽⋆−𝐛t=(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1​(∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s−λ​𝜽⋆).\bm{\theta}_{t}-\bm{\theta}^{\star}-\mathbf{b}_{t}=(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1}\Big(\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}-\lambda\bm{\theta}^{\star}\Big). (S14)

Now, we can bound the norm as below:

‖𝜽t−𝜽⋆−𝐛t‖𝐀t\displaystyle\|\bm{\theta}_{t}-\bm{\theta}^{\star}-\mathbf{b}_{t}\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t}}
=‖(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1​(∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s−λ​𝜽⋆)‖𝐀t\displaystyle=\|(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1}\Big(\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}-\lambda\bm{\theta}^{\star}\Big)\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t}}
=(∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s−λ​𝜽⋆)⊤​(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1/2​(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1/2​𝐀t​(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1/2​(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1/2​(∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s−λ​𝜽⋆)\displaystyle=\sqrt{\Big(\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}-\lambda\bm{\theta}^{\star}\Big)^{\top}(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1/2}(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1/2}\mathbf{A}_{t}(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1/2}(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1/2}\Big(\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}-\lambda\bm{\theta}^{\star}\Big)}
≤1κσ​(∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s−λ​𝜽⋆)⊤​(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1​(∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s−λ​𝜽⋆)\displaystyle\leq\sqrt{\frac{1}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\Big(\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}-\lambda\bm{\theta}^{\star}\Big)^{\top}(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1}\Big(\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}-\lambda\bm{\theta}^{\star}\Big)}
=1κσ​‖∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s−λ​𝜽⋆‖(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1\displaystyle=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\kappa_{\sigma}}}\Big\|\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}-\lambda\bm{\theta}^{\star}\Big\|_{(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1}}
≤1κσ​(‖∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s‖(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1+‖λ​𝜽⋆‖(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1)\displaystyle\leq\frac{1}{\sqrt{\kappa_{\sigma}}}\Big(\Big\|\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}\Big\|_{(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1}}+\Big\|\lambda\bm{\theta}^{\star}\Big\|_{(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1}}\Big)
≤1κσ​(1κσ​‖∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s‖𝐀t−1+‖λ​𝜽⋆‖(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t)−1)\displaystyle\leq\frac{1}{\sqrt{\kappa_{\sigma}}}\Big(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\kappa_{\sigma}}}\Big\|\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}\Big\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t}^{-1}}+\Big\|\lambda\bm{\theta}^{\star}\Big\|_{(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t})^{-1}}\Big)
≤1κσ​‖∑s=1tϵs​𝐳s‖𝐀t−1+λκσ​‖𝜽⋆‖2,\displaystyle\leq\frac{1}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\Big\|\sum_{s=1}^{t}\epsilon_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}\Big\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t}^{-1}}+\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{\kappa_{\sigma}}}\|\bm{\theta}^{\star}\|_{2}, (S15)

where the first inequality is due to λ​𝐈+𝐀^t⪯𝐀t\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t}\preceq\mathbf{A}_{t} in Eq. (S13), the second inequality is due to the triangle inequality, the third inequality is due to κσ​𝐀t⪯λ​𝐈+𝐀^t\kappa_{\sigma}\mathbf{A}_{t}\preceq\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t} in Eq. (S13), the fourth inequality is due to λ​𝐈+𝐀^t⪰λ​𝐈\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t}\succeq\lambda\mathbf{I}.

Now, we can apply Lemma 7 to bound the first term, and the fact that ‖𝜽⋆‖2≤M\|\bm{\theta}^{\star}\|_{2}\leq M to bound the second term in Eq. (S15). As such, we further have

‖𝜽t−𝜽⋆−𝐛t‖𝐀t\displaystyle\|\bm{\theta}_{t}-\bm{\theta}^{\star}-\mathbf{b}_{t}\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t}} ≤1κσ​(ν​2​log⁡det(𝐀t)1/2​det(λ​𝐈)−1/2δ+λ​M)\displaystyle\leq\frac{1}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\Big(\nu\sqrt{2\log\frac{\det(\mathbf{A}_{t})^{1/2}\det(\lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1/2}}{\delta}}+\sqrt{\lambda}M\Big)
≤1κσ​(ν​2​(d​log⁡(1+L​t2/λ)+log⁡(1/δ))+λ​M),\displaystyle\leq\frac{1}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\Big(\nu\sqrt{2(d\log(1+Lt^{2}/\lambda)+\log(1/\delta))}+\sqrt{\lambda}M\Big),

where we apply Lemma 5 and Lemma 6 for the last inequality. ∎

Now we are ready to prove the regret bound of Algorithm 2.

Proof of Theorem 5.

We first apply Lemma 1 to decompose the instantaneous latent reward gap into different parts and bound them individually. Specifically, there exists 𝝎⋆∈ℝp\bm{\omega}^{\star}\in\mathbb{R}^{p} such that

h​(𝐱t,ct⋆)−h​(𝐱t,ct)\displaystyle h(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}})-h(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}})
=𝜽0⊤​[𝐠​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎0)−𝐠​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎0)]​(𝝎⋆−𝝎0)+𝜽⋆⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)]\displaystyle=\bm{\theta}_{0}^{\top}[\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{0})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{0})](\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0})+\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]
=𝜽0⊤​[𝐠​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎0)−𝐠​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎0)]​(𝝎⋆−𝝎0)\displaystyle=\bm{\theta}_{0}^{\top}[\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{0})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{0})](\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0})
+𝜽t−1⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)]\displaystyle\quad+\bm{\theta}^{\top}_{t-1}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]
−(𝜽t−1−𝜽⋆)⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)].\displaystyle\quad-(\bm{\theta}_{t-1}-\bm{\theta}^{\star})^{\top}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]. (S16)

Now, we upper bound these three terms. We bound the first term as below:

𝜽0⊤​[𝐠​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎0)−𝐠​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎0)]​(𝝎⋆−𝝎0)\displaystyle\bm{\theta}_{0}^{\top}[\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{0})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{0})](\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0})
≤‖𝜽0‖2⋅‖𝐠​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎0)−𝐠​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎0)‖F⋅‖𝝎⋆−𝝎0‖2\displaystyle\leq\|\bm{\theta}_{0}\|_{2}\cdot\|\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{0})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{0})\|_{F}\cdot\|\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0}\|_{2}
≤ℓLip​‖𝜽0‖2⋅‖𝐱t,ct⋆−𝐱t,ct‖2⋅‖𝝎⋆−𝝎0‖2\displaystyle\leq\ell_{\text{Lip}}\|\bm{\theta}_{0}\|_{2}\cdot\|\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}}-\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}}\|_{2}\cdot\|\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0}\|_{2}
≤ℓLip⋅2​(2+d−1​log⁡(1/δ))⋅2⋅(𝐡−𝐡~)⊤​𝐇−1​(𝐡−𝐡~)/m\displaystyle\leq\ell_{\text{Lip}}\cdot 2(2+\sqrt{d^{-1}\log(1/\delta)})\cdot 2\cdot\sqrt{(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})^{\top}\mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})/m}
≤C4​ℓLip​m−1/2​d−1​log⁡(1/δ)​(𝐡−𝐡~)⊤​𝐇−1​(𝐡−𝐡~),\displaystyle\leq C_{4}\ell_{\text{Lip}}m^{-1/2}\sqrt{d^{-1}\log(1/\delta)(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})^{\top}\mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})}, (S17)

where C4>0C_{4}>0 is the absolute constant, the first inequality is due to Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, the second inequality is due to Assumption 5. For the third inequality, we bound ‖𝜽0‖\|\bm{\theta}_{0}\| based on its initialisation scheme, bound ‖𝐱t,ct⋆−𝐱t,ct‖2\|\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}}-\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}}\|_{2} due to Assumption 4, and bound ‖𝝎⋆−𝝎0‖2\|\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0}\|_{2} due to Lemma 1.

For the second term, we use the definition of the upper confidence bound in Algorithm 2, i.e., 𝜽t−1⊤​f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)+αt​‖f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1≤𝜽t−1⊤​f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)+αt​‖f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1\bm{\theta}^{\top}_{t-1}f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})+\alpha_{t}\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}\leq\bm{\theta}^{\top}_{t-1}f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})+\alpha_{t}\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}, to derive the following upper bound:

𝜽t−1⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)]≤αt​‖f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1−αt​‖f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1,\displaystyle\bm{\theta}^{\top}_{t-1}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]\leq\alpha_{t}\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}-\alpha_{t}\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}, (S18)

where αt\alpha_{t} is the upper bound derived from Lemma 9.

The last term can be bounded as below.

−(𝜽t−1−𝜽⋆)⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)]\displaystyle-(\bm{\theta}_{t-1}-\bm{\theta}^{\star})^{\top}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]
=−(𝜽t−1−𝜽⋆−𝐛t−1)⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)]\displaystyle=-(\bm{\theta}_{t-1}-\bm{\theta}^{\star}-\mathbf{b}_{t-1})^{\top}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]
−𝐛t−1⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)]\displaystyle\quad-\mathbf{b}_{t-1}^{\top}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]
≤‖𝜽t−1−𝜽⋆−𝐛t−1‖𝐀t−1⋅‖f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1\displaystyle\leq\|\bm{\theta}_{t-1}-\bm{\theta}^{\star}-\mathbf{b}_{t-1}\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}}\cdot\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}
+‖𝜽t−1−𝜽⋆−𝐛t−1‖𝐀t−1⋅‖f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1\displaystyle\quad+\|\bm{\theta}_{t-1}-\bm{\theta}^{\star}-\mathbf{b}_{t-1}\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}}\cdot\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}
+‖𝐛t−1‖2⋅‖f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖2\displaystyle\quad+\|\mathbf{b}_{t-1}\|_{2}\cdot\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{2}
≤αt​‖f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1+αt​‖f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1\displaystyle\leq\alpha_{t}\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}+\alpha_{t}\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}
+‖𝐛t−1‖2⋅‖f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖2,\displaystyle\quad+\|\mathbf{b}_{t-1}\|_{2}\cdot\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{2}, (S19)

where the first inequality is due to the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, and the second inequality is due to Lemma 9.

Summarising Eq. (S18) and Eq. (S19), we can derive that

𝜽t−1⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)]−(𝜽t−1−𝜽⋆)⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)]\displaystyle\bm{\theta}^{\top}_{t-1}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]-(\bm{\theta}_{t-1}-\bm{\theta}^{\star})^{\top}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]
≤2​αt​‖f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1+‖𝐛t−1‖2⋅‖f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖2.\displaystyle\leq 2\alpha_{t}\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}+\|\mathbf{b}_{t-1}\|_{2}\cdot\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{2}. (S20)

Now, we aim to further bound the second term in Eq. (S20). We first bound ‖𝐛t−1‖2\|\mathbf{b}_{t-1}\|_{2}. To apply Lemma 8, we bound the |δs||\delta_{s}| as below:

|δs|\displaystyle|\delta_{s}| =|σ​(𝜽⋆⊤​𝐳s+bs)−σ​(𝜽⋆⊤​𝐳s)|\displaystyle=|\sigma(\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}\mathbf{z}_{s}+b_{s})-\sigma(\bm{\theta}^{\star\top}\mathbf{z}_{s})|
≤Lσ​|bs|\displaystyle\leq L_{\sigma}|b_{s}|
=Lσ​|𝜽0⊤​𝐠​(𝐱s,cs;𝝎0)​(𝝎⋆−𝝎0)|\displaystyle=L_{\sigma}|\bm{\theta}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0})|
≤Lσ​‖𝜽0‖2⋅‖𝐠​(𝐱s,cs;𝝎0)‖F⋅‖𝝎⋆−𝝎0‖2\displaystyle\leq L_{\sigma}\|\bm{\theta}_{0}\|_{2}\cdot\|\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{s,c_{s}};\bm{\omega}_{0})\|_{F}\cdot\|\bm{\omega}^{\star}-\bm{\omega}_{0}\|_{2}
≤Lσ⋅2​(2+d−1​log⁡(1/δ))⋅C1​m​L​d⋅(𝐡−𝐡~)⊤​𝐇−1​(𝐡−𝐡~)/m\displaystyle\leq L_{\sigma}\cdot 2(2+\sqrt{d^{-1}\log(1/\delta)})\cdot C_{1}\sqrt{mLd}\cdot\sqrt{(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})^{\top}\mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})/m}
≤C5​Lσ​log⁡(1/δ)​L​(𝐡−𝐡~)⊤​𝐇−1​(𝐡−𝐡~),\displaystyle\leq C_{5}L_{\sigma}\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)L(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})^{\top}\mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})}, (S21)

where C5>0C_{5}>0 is an absolute constant, the first inequality is due to the definition of Lipschitz constant LσL_{\sigma}, the second inequality is due to triangle inequality, the third inequality is due to the initialisation scheme of 𝜽0\bm{\theta}_{0}, Lemma 1 and Lemma 3. Then, we can derive that

‖𝐛t−1‖2\displaystyle\|\mathbf{b}_{t-1}\|_{2} =‖(λ​𝐈+𝐀^t−1)−1​(∑s=1t−1δs​𝐳s)‖2\displaystyle=\|(\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t-1})^{-1}\big(\sum_{s=1}^{t-1}\delta_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}\big)\|_{2}
≤1κσ​‖𝐀t−1−1​(∑s=1t−1δs​𝐳s)‖2\displaystyle\leq\frac{1}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\|\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}\big(\sum_{s=1}^{t-1}\delta_{s}\mathbf{z}_{s}\big)\|_{2}
≤2​C5​Lσ​dκσ​log⁡(1/δ)​L​(𝐡−𝐡~)⊤​𝐇−1​(𝐡−𝐡~),\displaystyle\leq\frac{2C_{5}L_{\sigma}d}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)L(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})^{\top}\mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})}, (S22)

where the first inequality is due to the fact that λ​𝐈+𝐀^t−1⪰κσ​𝐀t−1\lambda\mathbf{I}+\widehat{\mathbf{A}}_{t-1}\succeq\kappa_{\sigma}\mathbf{A}_{t-1}, the second inequality is due to Lemma 8.

Next, we bound ‖f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖2\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{2} via Lemma 4:

‖f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖2\displaystyle\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{2}
=∥f(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−𝐠(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎0)(𝝎t−1−𝝎0)−f(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)+𝐠(𝐱t,ct;𝝎0)(𝝎t−1−𝝎0)\displaystyle=\Big\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}_{t-1}-\bm{\omega}_{0})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})+\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}_{t-1}-\bm{\omega}_{0})
+(𝐠​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎0)−𝐠​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎0))​(𝝎t−1−𝝎0)∥2\displaystyle\quad+(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{0})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{0}))(\bm{\omega}_{t-1}-\bm{\omega}_{0})\Big\|_{2}
≤‖f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−𝐠​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎0)​(𝝎t−1−𝝎0)‖2+‖f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)−𝐠​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎0)​(𝝎t−1−𝝎0)‖2\displaystyle\leq\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}_{t-1}-\bm{\omega}_{0})\|_{2}+\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{0})(\bm{\omega}_{t-1}-\bm{\omega}_{0})\|_{2}
+‖𝐠​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎0)−𝐠​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎0)‖F⋅‖𝝎t−1−𝝎0‖2\displaystyle\quad+\|\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{0})-\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{0})\|_{F}\cdot\|\bm{\omega}_{t-1}-\bm{\omega}_{0}\|_{2}
≤2​C3​m−1/6​d​log⁡m​(tλ)2/3​L3+2​ℓLip​t/(m​λ),\displaystyle\leq 2C_{3}m^{-1/6}\sqrt{d\log m}\Big(\frac{t}{\lambda}\Big)^{2/3}L^{3}+2\ell_{\text{Lip}}\sqrt{t/(m\lambda)}, (S23)

where the first inequality is due to triangle inequality, the second inequality is due to Lemma 4, Assumption 5, and Lemma 2.

Plugging Eq. (S22) and Eq. (S23) back into Eq. (S20) yields

𝜽t−1⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)]−(𝜽t−1−𝜽⋆)⊤​[f​(𝐱t,ct⋆;𝝎t−1)−f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)]\displaystyle\bm{\theta}^{\top}_{t-1}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]-(\bm{\theta}_{t-1}-\bm{\theta}^{\star})^{\top}[f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})-f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})]
≤2​αt​‖f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1+2​C5​Lσ​dκσ​log⁡(1/δ)​L​(𝐡−𝐡~)⊤​𝐇−1​(𝐡−𝐡~)\displaystyle\leq 2\alpha_{t}\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}+\frac{2C_{5}L_{\sigma}d}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)L(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})^{\top}\mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})}
⋅(2​C3​m−1/6​d​log⁡m​(tλ)2/3​L3+2​ℓLip​t/(m​λ)).\displaystyle\quad\cdot\Big(2C_{3}m^{-1/6}\sqrt{d\log m}\Big(\frac{t}{\lambda}\Big)^{2/3}L^{3}+2\ell_{\text{Lip}}\sqrt{t/(m\lambda)}\Big). (S24)

Now, we can derive the upper bound of the instantaneous latent reward gap as:

h​(𝐱t,ct⋆)−h​(𝐱t,ct)\displaystyle h(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}})-h(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}})
≤2​αt​‖f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−1+C4​ℓLip​m−1/2​d−1​log⁡(1/δ)​(𝐡−𝐡~)⊤​𝐇−1​(𝐡−𝐡~)\displaystyle\leq 2\alpha_{t}\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}+C_{4}\ell_{\text{Lip}}m^{-1/2}\sqrt{d^{-1}\log(1/\delta)(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})^{\top}\mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})}
+2​C5​Lσ​dκσ​log⁡(1/δ)​L​(𝐡−𝐡~)⊤​𝐇−1​(𝐡−𝐡~)\displaystyle\quad+\frac{2C_{5}L_{\sigma}d}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)L(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})^{\top}\mathbf{H}^{-1}(\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}})}
⋅(2​C3​m−1/6​d​log⁡m​(tλ)2/3​L3+2​ℓLip​t/(m​λ)).\displaystyle\quad\cdot\Big(2C_{3}m^{-1/6}\sqrt{d\log m}\Big(\frac{t}{\lambda}\Big)^{2/3}L^{3}+2\ell_{\text{Lip}}\sqrt{t/(m\lambda)}\Big). (S25)

Therefore, the regret of Neural-LinLogUCB can be bounded as:

RT\displaystyle R_{T} =∑t=1T(σ​(h​(𝐱t,ct⋆))−σ​(h​(𝐱t,ct)))\displaystyle=\sum_{t=1}^{T}\big(\sigma(h(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}}))-\sigma(h(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}}))\big)
≤∑t=1TLσ​|h​(𝐱t,ct⋆)−h​(𝐱t,ct)|\displaystyle\leq\sum_{t=1}^{T}L_{\sigma}|h(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}^{\star}})-h(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}})|
≤T​maxt∈[T]⁡αt2​∑t=1T‖f​(𝐱t,ct;𝝎t−1)‖𝐀t−1−12+C4​ℓLip​m−1/2​T​d−1​log⁡(1/δ)​‖𝐡−𝐡~‖𝐇−1\displaystyle\leq\sqrt{T\max_{t\in[T]}\alpha_{t}^{2}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|f(\mathbf{x}_{t,c_{t}};\bm{\omega}_{t-1})\|_{\mathbf{A}_{t-1}^{-1}}^{2}}+C_{4}\ell_{\text{Lip}}m^{-1/2}T\sqrt{d^{-1}\log(1/\delta)}\|\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}}\|_{\mathbf{H}^{-1}}
+2​C5​Lσ​dκσ​log⁡(1/δ)​L​‖𝐡−𝐡~‖𝐇−1\displaystyle\quad+\frac{2C_{5}L_{\sigma}d}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)L}\|\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}}\|_{\mathbf{H}^{-1}}
⋅(2​C3​m−1/6​T5/3​d​log⁡m​λ−2/3​L3+2​ℓLip​m−1/2​T3/2​λ−1/2)\displaystyle\quad\cdot\Big(2C_{3}m^{-1/6}T^{5/3}\sqrt{d\log m}\lambda^{-2/3}L^{3}+2\ell_{\text{Lip}}m^{-1/2}T^{3/2}\lambda^{-1/2}\Big)
≤C6κσ​T​d​log⁡(1+L​T2/λ2)​(ν​d​log⁡(1+L​T2/λ)+log⁡(1/δ)+λ​M)\displaystyle\leq\frac{C_{6}}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\sqrt{Td\log(1+LT^{2}/\lambda^{2})}\Big(\nu\sqrt{d\log(1+LT^{2}/\lambda)+\log(1/\delta)}+\sqrt{\lambda}M\Big)
+C7​dκσ​log⁡(1/δ)​L​‖𝐡−𝐡~‖𝐇−1\displaystyle\quad+\frac{C_{7}d}{\kappa_{\sigma}}\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)L}\|\mathbf{h}-\tilde{\mathbf{h}}\|_{\mathbf{H}^{-1}}
⋅(m−1/6​T5/3​d​log⁡m​λ−2/3​L3+ℓLip​m−1/2​T3/2​λ−1/2),\displaystyle\quad\cdot\Big(m^{-1/6}T^{5/3}\sqrt{d\log m}\lambda^{-2/3}L^{3}+\ell_{\text{Lip}}m^{-1/2}T^{3/2}\lambda^{-1/2}\Big),

where the first inequality is due to the definition of Lipschitz constant, the second inequality is due to Cauchy’s inequality, the third inequality comes from the fact that maxt∈[T]⁡αt=αT\max_{t\in[T]}\alpha_{t}=\alpha_{T}, Lemma 5 and Lemma 6, C6,C7>0C_{6},C_{7}>0 are absolute constants independent of other parameters. ∎

Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors. Generated by L A T E xml .

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.

BETA
